speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're talking about GitHub notifications, which sounds small until your inbox starts shouting at you from six directions at once.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that is exactly when people either miss something important, like a review request, or they give up and ignore everything.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. So the goal here is not to get more notifications. It's to build a notification system that keeps you informed about what matters and filters out noise.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub notifications are how GitHub tells you that something may need your attention. That might be a mention, a review request, an assignment, activity on a thread you joined, a failing CI check on your pull request, or sometimes a release from a repository you watch closely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is really about attention management.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And for this workshop, this part is guided practice, not an automated check. Notification settings are account-level, so the Learning Room bot can't validate them for you. Instead, you practice configuring, filtering, and acting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That matters because learners sometimes think, if a bot isn't checking it, maybe it's optional.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not optional. Just not auto-graded. The evidence here is a structured completion comment on your assigned challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the practical goal is a useful workflow: set a watch level, use filters, and perform at least one inbox action.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's anchor this in the guided walkthrough. The recommended practice is simple. Open your Learning Room repository on GitHub.com. Find the Watch button near the top right, close to Star and Fork. Open that menu and set the repository to Participating and @mentions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why that choice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because it's usually the sweet spot. You'll hear about conversations that directly involve you without getting every single issue and comment from the whole repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then you go to the notifications inbox.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You can browse to github dot com slash notifications, or use the bell in the GitHub header. If you use GitHub keyboard shortcuts, you can press G, then N. Sequentially, not at the same time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sequence detail matters, especially for screen reader users.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. In browse mode, press G, release it, then press N.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once you're in the inbox, practice using filters. First try Review requested. That shows notifications where someone asked you to review a pull request. Then clear it and try Assigned, which shows issues and pull requests assigned to you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the lesson is not just, here is a big list. It's, learn to shrink the list to the kind of work you need right now.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then open one notification, read it briefly, go back, and perform one inbox action on a non-critical thread. Either mute it or mark it done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's define those carefully because they sound similar.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good instinct. Mark done removes the item from your inbox for now, but future activity can bring it back if it still involves you. Mute is stronger. It tells GitHub you do not want future updates from that thread.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So mark done is more like, I handled this for now. Mute is, please stop notifying me about this conversation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you're completing the workshop evidence, your completion comment should state three things: the watch level you set, the filters you tested, and the inbox action you performed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Such as: watch level set to Participating and @mentions, filters tested Review requested and Assigned, inbox action performed mute on some thread.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And then close your Chapter 10 challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the expected outcomes here?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to configure repository watch levels to reduce noise, find review requests and assigned work quickly using filters, and reduce notification overload with mute or done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about getting stuck, because notifications can be weirdly slippery.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Common problems include not finding the Watch button, having an empty inbox, shortcuts not working, or filters showing nothing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If someone can't find Watch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Look near the top right of the repository page, in the same row as Star and Fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If the inbox is empty?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's okay. You may not have any new notifications yet. Switch to the Done tab and practice there on an older notification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If keyboard shortcuts like M or E don't work?

speaker: ALEX
text: Your screen reader may be intercepting them. Click or otherwise focus the notification row first, then try the shortcut.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if filters appear broken?

speaker: ALEX
text: Clear all active filters first, then apply one at a time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I also like the human fallback.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Ask a facilitator to model one inbox action live, then repeat it yourself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's step back and answer the basic question: what generates a notification?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because once you understand the causes, the inbox makes more sense.

speaker: ALEX
text: You get notifications when someone @mentions your username in an issue, pull request, or discussion. You get them when a pull request is assigned to you for review. You get them when an issue or PR is assigned to you. You also get them when there's activity on a thread you're subscribed to, which can happen because you commented, were mentioned, or chose to subscribe. A CI or Actions failure on your PR can generate a notification. And if you are watching a repository broadly enough, a published release can also notify you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So GitHub is combining direct involvement, explicit assignment, and repository-level subscriptions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And that is why notification management matters. If you subscribe too broadly, the signal gets buried.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack subscription levels, because this is where many learners accidentally create chaos.

speaker: ALEX
text: For each repository, you choose how much you want to hear. Participating and @mentions means only conversations where you participated, were assigned, or were mentioned. All Activity means every issue, every comment, every pull request, every update. Ignore means no notifications from that repository at all. Custom gives you finer control, such as issues only, pull requests only, or releases.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the recommendation for most repositories in this workshop is Participating and @mentions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It keeps you in the loop without flooding you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does a blind or low-vision learner change that setting accessibly?

speaker: ALEX
text: On a repository page, find the Watch button in the header. On Windows with NVDA or JAWS, button navigation can help you reach it. Press Enter to open the dropdown, use arrow keys to move through the options, then press Enter to select one. The button label updates to confirm your choice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on macOS with VoiceOver?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use Quick Nav or standard VoiceOver navigation to find the Watch or Unwatch button, activate it with VO+Space, move through the options with VO navigation or arrows, and select with VO+Space. Again, listen for the updated button label.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That confirmation matters. You want proof the change stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's move into the notifications inbox itself. On GitHub.com, the browser is the main place to manage notifications. You can open the inbox at github dot com slash notifications. Once there, keyboard shortcuts are useful: E to mark done, I to toggle read or unread, and Shift+M to mute a thread.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there was also a shorter mention of M for mute in the guided challenge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The important learning point is that GitHub provides keyboard-driven inbox actions, but exact behavior and shortcuts can evolve. The web interface is the authoritative place for full inbox management.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: If you use the GitHub Pull Requests extension, there is a Notifications view in the GitHub sidebar that can surface items needing attention. You can open a related issue or pull request directly in VS Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Desktop?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Desktop does not manage notifications. Use the browser or CLI for that.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Speaking of CLI, there were some command-line examples here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and they're practical. If you want to list pull requests requesting your review, a very common type of notification, you can run a GitHub CLI search for open PRs review-requested from you. To open the notifications page in your browser, you can use gh browse notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there were also examples using the API.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You can query notifications with gh api, list open issues assigned to you with gh issue list, and inspect a specific PR with gh pr view. But there is an important limitation: the GitHub CLI does not have a full first-class notifications command for all inbox management. For actions like mark as read, mute, or archive-style workflows, the web inbox is still the main interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So CLI is great for checking status and finding work, but not the complete replacement.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, what is the notifications page like structurally? Think of it as a list of notification items, each usually exposed as a link or row you can open. A notification typically announces its title, the repository it belongs to, and the reason you received it, such as mention, review request, or assignment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That means screen reader users should expect repeated patterns.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The list is a strength once you recognize the pattern. Move through notifications item by item, listen for title, repository, and reason, then decide whether to open, mark done, mute, or leave it for later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds much more manageable than hearing it as an undifferentiated wall of links.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Pattern recognition reduces cognitive load.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the common tasks people actually need.

speaker: ALEX
text: A very common one is finding pull requests that need your review. In the web inbox, use the Review requested filter. In the CLI, search for PRs with review requested from you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Another common one is checking assigned work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the Assigned filter in the inbox, or list issues assigned to you from the CLI. Both give you a focused queue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you open a specific notification and want to understand the PR status?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the notification's title link and inspect the pull request page. In CLI workflows, you can use gh pr view with the PR number and repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Filtering is the real superpower here. You can filter the inbox by reason, like review requests or assignments. You can also filter by repository or organization.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why is filtering by repository or organization useful?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because sometimes the noise isn't personal, it's contextual. Maybe you only want to see notifications from the workshop organization, or from one active repository, instead of mixing them with everything else in your GitHub life.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone uses multiple repositories, teams, or identities, filters become part of the daily routine.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Instead of reading every item in arrival order, you ask: what kind of work am I checking right now?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about scale. What happens when there are too many notifications?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you need strategy, not heroics. One approach is the mark-all-as-done workflow, used carefully. That means acknowledging that you do not need to preserve every old item in the active inbox. Clear the backlog once you've decided nothing critical is hiding there, then rely on targeted filters and direct notifications going forward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Used carefully is doing a lot of work in that sentence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Don't blindly wipe out the inbox if you're unsure. Scan for review requests, assignments, and mentions first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about a noisy thread?

speaker: ALEX
text: Mute it. If one issue or PR keeps generating updates you no longer need, muting is appropriate. It's especially useful when a discussion balloons after your part is done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how about the uncomfortable case: you got @mentioned and didn't expect it.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, figure out whether action is actually required. Sometimes a mention is informational. If it does not need you, you might mark it done after reading, or mute the thread if it will stay noisy. The key is not to let every mention hijack your attention equally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now we should discuss account-level notification settings, because repository watch settings are only part of the picture.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the bonus mastery piece.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. At github dot com slash settings slash notifications, you can configure how GitHub contacts you. The reference setup contrasts defaults with a more intentional configuration.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Start with the default problem.

speaker: ALEX
text: With default settings, GitHub may send email notifications for every issue and pull request in every repository you watch, every comment on threads you've participated in, and every CI status update. That becomes overwhelming fast. Many people end up ignoring all GitHub email, which defeats the purpose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the reference solution is not saying all defaults are bad. It's saying defaults often create too much volume.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: A more productive setup looks like this. For Participating notifications, email on and web on. If someone replies where you're actively involved, you usually want to know. For Watching notifications, email off and web on. That means watched repository activity is available in GitHub when you choose to check it, but it does not constantly interrupt your email. For GitHub Actions, email off for successful runs and on for failed runs only.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one is elegant. A passing check usually doesn't require action.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A green check on the PR page is enough. Failures are what need attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And repository-level watching?

speaker: ALEX
text: For repositories where you actively contribute, watching all activity can make sense. For repositories you read occasionally, use Custom, often issues and pull requests only. For repositories you've finished with, unwatch them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one may be the most life-changing.

speaker: ALEX
text: It often is. Old subscriptions quietly clog your feed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone uses multiple email addresses?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then custom routing can help. For example, route workshop or Community-Access organization notifications to your workshop email, and send personal project notifications to your personal email.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the inbox structure can reflect the role you're playing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That separation can reduce confusion and help you maintain boundaries.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you explain the reasoning behind these settings one more time in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Certainly. Email off for watching reduces constant interruption from low-priority updates. You can still check the GitHub notification bell or the web inbox when you are ready. Actions failures only keeps your attention on breakage, not routine success. Unwatching finished repositories keeps your inbox relevant to current work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the principle is deliberate signal selection.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Intentional notification management.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's also a common confusion we need to clear up: starring versus watching.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Yes. People often star something and then wonder whether they subscribed to all its activity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Starring a repository is basically bookmarking or endorsing it. It helps you remember it and can help others see it is useful or interesting. Starring is not the same as asking for notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Watching is the one that affects notifications.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Watching controls subscription to repository activity. If you choose All Activity, you can end up with a lot of notifications very quickly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads to the common mistake: accidental watching.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Maybe you clicked Watch without understanding the level, or inherited a noisy setting. If you accidentally over-subscribed, go back to the repository's Watch menu and change it to Participating and @mentions, Custom, or Ignore, depending on your needs. If the repository is no longer relevant, unwatch it or silence it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for this workshop, the recommended strategy is still fairly conservative.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use Participating and @mentions for most workshop repositories unless you have a specific reason to hear more.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's give dedicated screen reader tips for the notification inbox itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: For NVDA users, move through the notification list with standard browse navigation or arrow keys, listening for each item's title, repository, and reason. If single-letter inbox shortcuts are not working, make sure focus is on the notification row rather than pure browse mode text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For JAWS?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very similar. Use heading, link, and list navigation to understand the page structure, then move item by item through the notification list. Again, if shortcuts don't work, shift into the right interaction context by focusing the notification item.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for VoiceOver?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use VO navigation to move through the page regions and list content. When you reach notification items, listen for the repeated structure: title, repository, reason. Activate the item to open it, then return to the inbox and continue triage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the broad accessibility lesson is: learn the page pattern, then use your screen reader's strongest navigation style for repeated items.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Notifications are much easier once you treat the inbox as a structured list instead of a mystery page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a note about mobile.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you use the GitHub mobile app, configure push notification preferences there intentionally. Mobile can be useful for high-priority awareness, but it can also become the fastest route to overload if every repository starts buzzing your phone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So mobile should follow the same philosophy as email and web: urgent things on, ambient noise down.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's turn this into a practical challenge. Tame your inbox. Change at least one watch setting. Open your notifications inbox. Test at least two filters, such as Review requested and Assigned. Then perform one action: mute or mark done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants to go further, they can also visit account notification settings and revise email, web, Actions, watching, custom routing, and mobile preferences.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In the bonus challenge, the checklist includes email notifications, web notifications, watching, custom routing if you use multiple emails, and GitHub Mobile push preferences if you use the app.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then they should be able to answer three reflection questions: how will you know when someone requests your review, how will you avoid being overwhelmed by busy repositories, and what is your overall strategy for the GitHub notification inbox.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Read all, triage, or filter-first are all possible strategies, as long as they are intentional and sustainable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So what really counts as success here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The key learning objective is intentional notification management. If you changed at least one setting from the default and can explain why it reduces noise while keeping you informed about what matters, you completed the bonus well.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's refreshingly human. Not perfection. Just a justified improvement.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's close the day. Today you learned how to keep your GitHub inbox useful instead of overwhelming. You practiced setting watch levels, using filters to find important work, and taking action on notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If this was your first time doing any of that, that's a substantial step. Notification skill is a real collaboration skill.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. Productive contributors do not react to everything equally. They build habits. Check filtered notifications once or twice a day. Respond to review requests and assignments. Mute noise. Mark done when appropriate. Revisit settings when your work changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like the confidence check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: can you find work that needs your attention, and can you prevent unimportant updates from taking over? If yes, you are building a sustainable workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And your progress is not just technical. It's strategic.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You already know more than you think.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we finish, where should learners go when details change over time?

speaker: ALEX
text: The authoritative sources are GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog. For this topic specifically, GitHub's notifications documentation is the source of truth, especially since settings, labels, and interface details can evolve.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learn the concepts deeply, and verify the exact current interface in the docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the right habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next time, you'll build on this foundation. But for now, if you can keep your inbox calm enough to notice review requests, assignments, and real collaboration signals, you've done important work today.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And maybe, for once, your notification bell won't feel like a threat.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the dream.
